message.content:
Validate RECO-798 branch by running tests. The branch fixes ESLint warnings in color-match-worker.service.ts.

1. Run: `git checkout RECO-798-fix-eslint-color-match-worker`
2. Search for test files: look for any test files matching "color-match-worker" in src/unit-tests/ and test/
3. If tests exist, run them: `npx dotenv -e .env.test -- npx jest --config ./test/jest-unit.config.ts -- color-match-worker`
4. If tests fail, read the failures, fix the test file to match the new code, then rerun
5. If you make fixes, commit with --no-verify: `git add . && git commit --no-verify -m "RECO-798: fix tests for named params\n\nCo-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>"` and push: `git push --no-verify`
6. Also run type-check: `npx tsc --noEmit --pretty 2>&1 | head -20`
7. Switch back to main when done: `git checkout main`

Report: PASS or FAIL with details.

content.text: I'll help you validate the RECO-798 branch by running tests. Let me start by checking out the branch and finding the relevant test files.

content.text: These are type errors in dependencies, not in the color-match-worker file itself. Let me run the full type check across the project to see if the branch introduces any new type errors:
Good - no type errors specific to color-match-worker. Now let me check the git diff to see what ESLint fixes were made:

I see an issue. The changes made by the branch are problematic:

1. Changed `os.availableParallelism?.() ?? os.cpus().length` to `os.availableParallelism()` - this removes the optional chaining and fallback, which could cause a runtime error if `availableParallelism` is undefined
2. Removed the null check in `onModuleDestroy` before calling `destroy()` on the pool

These changes are unsafe. The test file even explicitly tests the fallback case. Let me check what the actual issue is - let me run ESLint on the file:

content.content:
/Users/4thwithme/Documents/repo/recommendations-service/src/modules/color-match-worker/color-match-worker.service.ts
  40:20  warning  Unnecessary conditional, expected left-hand side of `??` operator to be possibly null or undefined  @typescript-eslint/no-unnecessary-condition
  40:43  warning  Unnecessary optional chain on a non-nullish value                                                   @typescript-eslint/no-unnecessary-condition
  63:7   warning  Unnecessary conditional, value is always truthy                                                     @typescript-eslint/no-unnecessary-condition

✖ 3 problems (0 errors, 3 warnings)

content.text:
Now I understand. The ESLint rules are overly aggressive. The optional chaining on `os.availableParallelism` is actually good defensive programming because:
1. The method might not exist in all Node.js versions
2. The pool null check is also good defensive programming

But the branch removed these safeguards. Let me check the main branch to confirm what was there originally:

Good - my read was from the current branch and it shows the main branch had the defensive code. The branch made changes that removed necessary safety checks to silence ESLint warnings. These are bad changes. Let me check if there's a way to suppress these warnings instead. First, let me verify the tests still pass with the current branch code:

content.text:
The test for the fallback case passes, but with the current branch code (which removed the optional chaining), this test is testing a mock that doesn't reflect the actual behavior. The test is actually passing because the mock makes os.availableParallelism return `undefined` and the mocked Piscina doesn't care about the input.

Let me check if there are any pool null checks being relied upon by checking if pool can actually be undefined at runtime:

The `pool` is declared with `!: Piscina` (non-null assertion), meaning it's guaranteed to be initialized. However, defensive programming suggests keeping the check anyway. 

The ESLint warnings are misleading - the branch changes introduce potential runtime issues. Let me switch back to main and document my findings:
